title: New tensor categories : work of N. Harman, S. Kriz, A. Snowden, N. Snyder ...
speaker: Pierre Deligne
abstract: Pretannakian categories, that is k-linear abelian categories with finite dimensional Hom groups, given with a commutative and associative tensor product, with a unit object (such that End(1)=k) and duals, can be viewed as generalizations of linear algebraic groups over k : to G corresponds the category Rep(G) of representations of G, and for k algebraically closed, G can be recovered (up to inner automorphisms) from the category. In characteristic 0, for a long time, the only known pretannakian categories were obtained by "interpolation" from categories Rep(G). This changed a few years ago, with a new point of view leading to unexpected beautiful such categories.

proof_stages.label: Pretannakian categories as generalized symmetry groups
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up rigid symmetric tensor categories with duals, fiber functors, and categorical dimensions, explaining via the Tannakian dictionary why Pretannakian categories generalize representation categories of algebraic groups.

proof_stages.label: Deligne interpolation of symmetric-group representations
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by nonintegral dimension, the speaker recalls Deligne’s construction of Rep(S_t): finite symmetric-group homogeneous spaces have orbit-indexed Hom bases and polynomial composition constants in |I|, so |I| can be replaced by a parameter t and then completed to a Pretannakian category.

proof_stages.label: Oligomorphic-action reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The finite symmetric-group interpolation is reformulated using a single infinite group action, where an oligomorphic action has finitely many orbits on all finite powers and hence supplies finite orbit data for objects and Hom spaces, but composition involves infinite sums.

proof_stages.label: Measures on orbit maps and associativity
proof_stages.summary: To regularize the infinite composition sums, one assigns a measure, or generalized fiber cardinality, to equivariant maps between homogeneous spaces, with identity, multiplicativity under composition, and pullback/additivity axioms that force the resulting kernel composition to be associative.

proof_stages.label: Ordered-set Euler-characteristic category
proof_stages.summary: For the automorphism group of a dense ordered set, the orbits are finite ordered sets with increasing injections, and compactly supported Euler characteristic of cell fibers gives a measure producing a new tensor category whose objects are self-dual, have dimensions ±1, and whose Grothendieck λ-ring has identity Adams operations.

proof_stages.label: Complete-flag finite-field vector-space category
proof_stages.summary: A second construction replaces the ordered set by an infinite-dimensional vector space over a finite field equipped with a complete flag; its automorphism group is oligomorphic, with orbits indexed by finite-dimensional flagged vector spaces, and a q-dependent measure is supported by a dimension-minus-one heuristic.

proof_stages.label: Semisimplicity criteria and classification outlook
proof_stages.summary: In the questions, the speaker explains that measure relations can be organized into a ring and that a criterion of Harman, Snowden, and Snyder gives semisimplicity when there are enough maps to a field and no zero-dimensional objects, while broad classification of semisimple Pretannakian categories remains far out of reach.

examples.note: SL_N and SO_N are cited as familiar linear algebraic groups whose representation categories instantiate the Tannakian dictionary between tensor categories with fiber functors and affine group schemes.

examples.name: Dimensional regularization of divergent integrals
examples.note: The physics practice of evaluating formulas in a variable dimension d and then extracting a value near dimension four is used to motivate the idea of defining representation-theoretic objects at nonintegral dimension.
examples.engagement_reason: Dimensional regularization is described conceptually as assigning finite parts by varying dimension, but no specific divergent integral is evaluated.

examples.name: Vogel-style patterns among simple Lie groups
examples.note: The uniform decompositions and rational dimension formulas across the classical and exceptional simple Lie series illustrate the temptation to seek tensor categories depending on a continuous parameter, although the speaker says this route breaks down.
examples.engagement_reason: Vogel-style Lie-group patterns are summarized by stating uniform decompositions and dimension formulas, without deriving them in a particular case.

examples.name: One-dimensional flagged vector-space orbit
examples.note: In the case of a one-dimensional flagged vector space, the orbit is the nonzero vectors of the infinite flagged space, and the computation heuristically derives the measure as q^{-1}-1 by treating the ordered index set as having cardinality -1.
examples.engagement_reason: For the one-dimensional flagged vector-space orbit, the speaker traces the orbit as nonzero vectors in omega and heuristically computes its measure as 1/q minus 1.
